concerns.text: The validation design is circular. FR-005 computes Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between topic distributions derived from the SAME LDA model (or same training regime) applied to different windows. FR-007 then uses bootstrap resampling of these SAME distributions to generate a 'null distribution' for significance testing. In standard topic modeling, the 'null' of no drift is not generated by resampling the observed topic vectors; it requires a permutation test (shuffling abstracts across time windows) or a generative null model. Resampling the observed vectors (FR-007) creates a confidence interval around the *observed* drift, not a test against *no drift*. This makes the p-value a tautology of the observed data, not a statistical test of the hypothesis.

The validation target (coherence score) is not independent of the predictor construction. FR-003 fixes k=10 and max_iter=20. FR-006 discards runs if coherence < 0.4. This creates a selection bias where the 'valid' topic vectors used for drift calculation (FR-005) are those that happen to fit the data well under the specific k=10 constraint. If the field has no 10 distinct topics, the model forces them, and the resulting 'drift' is an artifact of the forced decomposition, not a real phenomenon. The spec lacks a mechanism to validate that k=10 is the correct number of topics (e.g., via elbow method or held-out likelihood) before accepting the drift measurement.

The preprocessing pipeline (tokenization, stopword removal, lemmatization) is applied uniformly across 24 years of text (2000-2024). This violates the assumption of stationarity required for valid temporal comparison. Stopword lists and lemmatization rules evolve with language and domain jargon. A word treated as a stopword in 2000 might be a key term in 2024 (or vice versa). Applying a static preprocessing pipeline (FR-002) introduces systematic noise that mimics topic drift. The spec must either use dynamic, window-specific preprocessing or justify why a static pipeline does not bias the JS divergence metric.
